title: Chapter 9 — Privacy, Student Data, and Institutional Boundaries
CHAPTER 9 —
PRIVACY, STUDENT DATA, AND INSTITUTIONAL BOUNDARIES
segments.body_html:
It is Thursday afternoon and you have seventeen advising notes open in a browser tab, a stack of accommodation letters f

Chapter 9 — Privacy, Student Data, and I — Before you type anything, stop.

beats.text: It is Thursday afternoon and you have seventeen advising notes open in a browser tab, a stack of accommodation letters from the disability services office, and a student whose writing has suddenly deteriorated in ways that suggest something is wrong at home. You want to do something useful. You open Claude. This chapter is about what happens in the pause before the prompt. Most AI-in-education discussions rush past that pause. This one does not, because the pause is where your students are protected or exposed.

segments.label: 01 / 11 · HOOK

segments.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO
title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
Apply data minimizationask Claude for help with the pattern, not the person.
·Anonymize or aggregate student information before any external tool touches it.
·Identify the regulated categories your institution handles and why they require extra caution.
·Run a five-question policy check before using Claude with student-adjacent content.
·Know which tasks should go to your institution's IT, legal, or data-governance team rather than to Claude.

beats.text: By the end of this chapter you will be able to: Apply data minimization: ask Claude for help with the pattern, not the person. Anonymize or aggregate student information before any external tool touches it. Identify the regulated categories your institution handles and why they require extra caution. Run a five-question policy check before using Claude with student-adjacent content. Know which tasks should go to your institution's IT, legal, or data-governance team rather than to Claude.

segments.label: 02 / 11 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: CORE CONCEPTS AND
TERMS
segments.body_html:
·Grades and academic records (FERPA-covered in the U.S.)
·Disability documentation and accommodation letters
·Counseling and mental health records (often separately protected)
·Immigration status documentation
·Financial aid and billing records

beats.text: Data minimization means giving Claude only the information it needs to assist with the task , nothing more. If you are asking Claude to suggest language for a mid-semester check-in email, it does not need the student's name, course grade, accommodation status, or personal history. It needs the situation type and the tone you want. Minimization is the first line of defense. Anonymization means removing or replacing identifying details before sharing information with an external system.

segments.label: 03 / 11 · CORE CONCEPTS AND TERMS

title: THE FIVE-QUESTION
POLICY CHECK.
segments.body_html: Is any individual student identifiable in what I am about to share?→Does this involve a regulated category?→Has my institution approved this tool for this data type?→Is there a way to complete the task without including the sensitive detail?→Where will the output go, and is that appropriate?

beats.text: Before using Claude in a workflow that involves any student information , even something that feels minor , ask five questions. If you cannot answer all five clearly, consult your institution before proceeding. Is any individual student identifiable in what I am about to share? Names, student ID numbers, email addresses, and specific combinations of details can all identify someone. If yes, either anonymize or do not proceed until policy is clear.

segments.label: 04 / 11 · THE FIVE-QUESTION POLICY CHECK

beats.text: Consider the following scenario , realistic, and common. A high school teacher has three students who are struggling significantly. She wants to use Claude to draft differentiated support plans. She has their names, their current grades, their IEP documentation, and notes from a conversation with the school counselor.

title: WHAT CLAUDE CANNOT KNOW —
AND THE TEACHER MUST HOLD
segments.body_html:
Even with a data-minimization workflow, some things cannot and should not be delegated. When you know a student is in cr

What Claude Cannot Know — and the Teache — Claude does not know that student. Claude cannot judge whether an accommodation plan is appropriate for their actual sit

beats.text: Even with a data-minimization workflow, some things cannot and should not be delegated. When you know a student is in crisis , struggling at home, dealing with housing instability, navigating immigration status, managing a mental health episode , that knowledge came to you through a professional relationship, a system of trust, and sometimes a legal obligation of confidentiality. Claude does not know that student. Claude cannot judge whether an accommodation plan is appropriate for their actual situation.

segments.label: 06 / 11 · WHAT CLAUDE CANNOT KNOW — AND

title: THE HUMAN
GATE.
segments.body_html:
For every workflow involving student-adjacent content, the human gate has two parts.

The Human Gate — Before Claude: Did I minimize the data? Did I anonymize? Did I check policy? If no to any of these, stop or consult.

beats.text: For every workflow involving student-adjacent content, the human gate has two parts. Before Claude: Did I minimize the data? Did I anonymize? Did I check policy? If no to any of these, stop or consult.

segments.label: 07 / 11 · THE HUMAN GATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Mistake 1: Assuming "I didn't name them" is enough.→Mistake 2: Treating institutional approval as obvious.→Mistake 3: Storing AI outputs carelessly.→Mistake 4: Skipping the check because the task feels minor.→Mistake 5: Using Claude as a diagnostic authority.

beats.text: Mistake 1: Assuming "I didn't name them" is enough. Aggregated details can re-identify students, especially in small classes or small institutions. "The only student in the class who uses a wheelchair and is failing calculus" is identifiable even without a name. Think about combinations, not just individual fields. Mistake 2: Treating institutional approval as obvious. Some institutions have enterprise AI agreements that cover certain tools for certain data types. Many do not. Verify before you build a workflow that assumes approval.

segments.label: 08 / 11 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE TO

Bridge to Chapter 10 — The underlying question is the same in both chapters: whose judgment is doing the work? In this chapter, that judgment b

beats.text: Privacy is a constraint that operates before the student ever opens an AI tool. The next chapter turns to what happens when they do: how to design student-facing AI-use policies that are teachable, enforceable, and grounded in learning rather than fear. The underlying question is the same in both chapters: whose judgment is doing the work? In this chapter, that judgment belongs to you and your institution. In Chapter 10, you are designing conditions for students to develop judgment of their own.

segments.label: 09 / 11 · BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 10
